title: Is Legal Advice Free at the Police Station? | PoliceStationRepUK

headings.text: Is Legal Advice Free at the Police Station?
Yes — Free Legal Aid with No Means Test
Can the Police Discourage You from Getting a Solicitor?
How to Request Free Legal Advice

content:
One of the most common questions people have when they are arrested or asked to attend a voluntary police interview is whether they will have to pay for a solicitor. The answer is straightforward: legal advice at the police station is completely free under the Legal Aid scheme, and there is no means test.

Yes — Free Legal Aid with No Means Test

Under the Legal Aid, Sentencing and Punishment of Offenders Act 2012 and the Police and Criminal Evidence Act 1984 (PACE), every person detained at a police station or attending for a voluntary interview has the right to free and independent legal advice. Unlike other types of Legal Aid (such as for court proceedings), there is no financial eligibility test for police station advice. It does not matter what your income is, whether you own property, or what savings you have. The advice is funded by the Legal Aid Agency and is free for everyone.

How the Funding Works

When you request a solicitor at the police station, the Defence Solicitor Call Centre (DSCC) is contacted. The DSCC allocates the case either to your chosen solicitor (if you request one by name) or to the duty solicitor for that area. The solicitor or their accredited representative then attends the police station and provides advice. The solicitor claims the cost of this attendance from the Legal Aid Agency — you are never billed. The representative or solicitor is paid a fixed fee (or an escape fee for longer attendances) by Legal Aid. This system ensures that cost is never a barrier to receiving legal advice when you need it most.

What Free Legal Advice Covers

The free legal advice at the police station covers everything you need during your time in custody or at a voluntary interview. This includes a private consultation with a solicitor or accredited representative before your interview, advice on the evidence (disclosure) the police have, explanation of the caution and your rights (including the right to silence), attendance during all police interviews, representations on bail, detention, and charge, and any follow-up advice needed before you leave the police station. The solicitor or representative will also provide a detailed attendance note to their firm, which forms the basis for any further legal work if the case proceeds to court.

Voluntary Interviews — Still Free

If the police have contacted you and asked you to attend a voluntary interview (sometimes called an interview under caution), you have exactly the same right to free legal advice. Many people assume that because they have not been arrested, the advice might not be covered by Legal Aid. This is wrong. Voluntary interviews are funded in the same way as post-arrest interviews. You should always arrange legal representation before attending.

Can the Police Discourage You from Getting a Solicitor?

No. Under PACE Code C, the custody officer must inform you of your right to legal advice and must not do anything to discourage you from exercising that right. If you ask for a solicitor, the police must facilitate that request before any interview takes place (with very limited exceptions for urgent cases). If you feel pressured to proceed without a solicitor, this may be a breach of PACE that could affect the admissibility of your interview.

How to Request Free Legal Advice

If you have been arrested, simply tell the custody sergeant that you want a solicitor. You can name a specific firm or solicitor if you have one, or the duty solicitor will be arranged for you. If you are attending a voluntary interview, contact a criminal solicitor in advance. You can use the PoliceStationRepUK directory to find accredited representatives and solicitors in your area. There is never any cost to you for police station legal advice.
